fact: March 31 is the last day of the first quarter of the year - you've officially survived 25% of the year!
In the Northern Hemisphere, March 31 falls right in the sweet spot where days are getting noticeably longer - you gain about 2-3 minutes of daylight every single day around this time!
In northern climates, earthworms typically become very active around March 31 as the soil warms up enough for them to wiggle to the surface and start their important job of enriching the dirt.
Cherry blossoms in Japan often reach peak bloom around March 31, creating pink snow when the petals fall - but each individual flower only lasts about one week!
March 31 sunsets often look extra orange because the atmosphere still has winter dust and particles that scatter blue light, leaving behind those fire-colored evening skies.

Hibernating animals like groundhogs and chipmunks are typically waking up super hungry around March 31 in northern regions - they can lose up to 30% of their body weight during their long winter naps!
March 31 sits exactly 10 days after the spring equinox, which means the Earth has tilted just enough that the North Pole is starting to peek out of its 6-month darkness.
In North America, March 31 is prime maple syrup season when people tap maple trees to collect their sugary sap!
In Australia and the Southern Hemisphere, March 31 marks the beginning of autumn weather - leaves are turning colors and animals are preparing for their winter!
The moon on March 31 can appear extra bright and clear because spring air typically has less water vapor than summer air - it's like nature cleaned the sky's windows!
March 31 falls right in the middle of Aries season in the zodiac calendar, when the sun is in the constellation Aries.
March 31 is exactly 275 days away from Christmas - far enough that you've forgotten about your New Year's resolutions but close enough to start planning your next birthday party!
